description: Even the most tangled knot has its threads.
goal: (-> (and (or A B) (-> A C)) (or C B))

hints.text: This one brings everything together. The goal is an implication whose left side is a conjunction of two things: $A \lor B$ and $A \to C$. The right side is $C \lor B$. You'll need $\land$-elimination, $\lor$-introduction, $\to$-elimination, and $\lor$-elimination. Start by assuming the whole left side.
Assume the entire conjunction: $A \lor B$ **and** $A \to C$.
A conjunction tells you both halves are true. Break it open with `and-elim-l` and `and-elim-r` to get $A \lor B$ and $A \to C$ individually.
First, extract $A \lor B$ from the left side of the conjunction.
Now extract $A \to C$ from the right side.
You have a disjunction ($A \lor B$) and you need to reach $C \lor B$. Proof by cases is the natural move. Handle the $A$ case first: assume $A$.
From $A \to C$ at step 3 and $A$ at step 4, apply modus ponens to get $C$.
From $C$, form $C \lor B$ by slotting $C$ on the left of $\lor$ with $B$ on the right.
From $B$, we also need $C \lor B$. This time $B$ goes on the right — use `or-intro-r` with $C$ on the left.
Both cases — $A$ and $B$ — led to $C \lor B$. Use `or-elim` with the disjunction at step 2 and your two case-proofs at steps 6 and 8.
You proved $C \lor B$ from the conjunction. Discharge the assumption to finish the implication.
